items.q: Is utilitarianism just 'do whatever makes you happy'?
items.correct: No - it demands impartial maximisation of happiness for all affected, which can require serious personal sacrifice; the agent's own pleasure counts no more than anyone else's.
items.misconception: Utilitarianism is a selfish philosophy of personal pleasure-seeking.
items.random_wrong: Utilitarianism commands obedience to religious authority.
items.why: Confusing hedonism with impartial welfare-maximisation makes every evaluation of the theory misfire.

items.q: Is situation ethics the same as having no rules at all?
items.correct: No - Fletcher rejected both legalism and antinomianism; rules are valuable 'illuminators' that should be set aside only when agape love requires it.
items.misconception: Fletcher taught that moral rules are worthless and anything goes.
items.random_wrong: Situation ethics requires obeying every rule without exception.
items.why: Situation ethics occupies a deliberate middle position; collapsing it into lawlessness misses Fletcher's own framing.

items.q: Does natural law mean 'doing whatever comes naturally'?
items.correct: No - Aquinas meant rational reflection on the purposes of human nature; some instinctive urges are contrary to reason and therefore against natural law.
items.misconception: Natural moral law tells us to follow our instincts.
items.random_wrong: Natural law is the scientific study of gravity and motion.
items.why: The word 'natural' misleads; for Aquinas reason, not instinct, discerns the precepts.

items.q: What does Bentham's hedonic calculus measure?
items.correct: The quantity of pleasure and pain produced, using factors such as intensity, duration and certainty
items.misconception: The cost of an action in money
items.why: The answer examiners want is: The quantity of pleasure and pain produced, using factors such as intensity, duration and certainty. “The cost of an action in money” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: For Fletcher's situation ethics, what is the only thing intrinsically good?
items.why: The answer examiners want is: Agape love. “Pleasure” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: Which of these is one of Aquinas's primary precepts?
items.why: It's The preservation of life. “The maximisation of pleasure” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: What does jus ad bellum concern?
items.correct: Whether going to war is justified in the first place
items.misconception: How soldiers must behave during a war
items.random_wrong: How to rebuild a country after a war
items.why: It's Whether going to war is justified in the first place. “How soldiers must behave during a war” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.
